perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information belonging to a party or a related company (as hereinafter defined) which is not generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of which is not generally known or recognized as a standard practice and may include without limitation any and all financial information; any and all employment information; any and all technical and non-technical information, including patent, copyright, trade secret and similar proprietary information; any information related to current, future and proposed business information, plans, activities, products and services, computer software, and other technology, including without limitation, forecasts, market research, development, design details, specifications, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, contractor and subcontractor lists, and sales and merchandising plans (including such information of each and any affiliate, subsidiary, or the like) in any medium whatsoever, whether oral, written, machine readable data, through facsimile, electronic mail, postal service or otherwise, provided by or disclosed either directly or indirectly by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party whether such information is designated as confidential at the time of delivery or not. The term “Confidential Information” as used herein shall not include information: (I) which was generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of such information is already generally known or recognized as a standard practice at the time it was communicated to the Receiving Party or subsequently becomes generally available or recognized as a standard practice through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (ii) which the Receiving Party can demonstrate by a written or electronic document to have had rightfully in its possession free from any obligation of confidence at the time of disclosure; (iii) which the Receiving Party can demonstrate that it rightfully obtained free of any obligation of confidence subsequent to the time it was disclosed to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (iv) which the Receiving Party rightfully obtained from a third party who has the-right to transfer or disclose it ; (v) which the Receiving Party to whom it is disclosed hereunder can demonstrate was independently developed by such party or agents of that party without any use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information belonging to a party or a related company (as hereinafter defined) which is not generally available to or used by others. For clarity, this definition is strictly limited to only financial records explicitly marked as 'Confidential' and excludes any technical, employment, or business information even if not publicly available. The term “Confidential Information” as used herein shall not include information: (I) which was generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of such information is already generally known or recognized as a standard practice at the time it was communicated to the Receiving Party or subsequently becomes generally available or recognized as a standard practice through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (ii) which the Receiving Party can demonstrate by a written or electronic document to have had rightfully in its possession free from any obligation of confidence at the time of disclosure; (iii) which the Receiving Party can demonstrate that it rightfully obtained free of any obligation of confidence subsequent to the time it was disclosed to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (iv) which the Receiving Party rightfully obtained from a third party who has the-right to transfer or disclose it ; (v) which the Receiving Party to whom it is disclosed hereunder can demonstrate was independently developed by such party or agents of that party without any use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad, including financial, employment, technical, and business information. The modified definition restricts 'Confidential Information' solely to financial records explicitly marked as 'Confidential.' This creates a contradiction because later sections relying on the original definition will now be unclear if they apply to technical or business information that is not explicitly marked confidential. For example, the non-disclosure obligations would be weaker for certain categories of information, leading to uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use reasonable care, but in no event less than the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information. Each party shall only have the right to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees, agents and consultants on a “need to know” basis; provided, however, that disclosure in any event shall only be made to such persons who have agreed in writing to protect the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party’s information. Each party shall, prior to disclosing any Confidential Information to any such person, issue appropriate instructions to them and obtain all necessary undertakings to ensure that such Persons comply with the confidentiality and use obligations and restrictions contained In this Agreement with respect to the Confidential Information. These obligations shall survive termination or expiry of this Agreement. The Receiving Party may Disclose Confidential Information if required by law, provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior written notice of the requirement and provide reasonable assistance to enable the Disclosing Party to seek protection of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, including financial, employment, technical, and business information. The changed text narrows the definition to only financial records explicitly marked 'Confidential'. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which requires the Receiving Party to protect 'Confidential Information' with the same degree of care it uses to protect its own confidential information, and allows disclosure on a 'need to know' basis. Since 'Confidential Information' is now narrowly defined in the changed text, the Receiving Party's obligation to protect other types of information (employment, technical, business) is undermined, causing a conflict. The original text places obligations on *all* Confidential Information, while the changed text limits the scope of what is considered confidential, thus the change creates a strong in-text contradiction as it makes the confidentiality obligations in the 'Contradicted Text' unclear and unenforceable for broad categories of information previously covered.

perturbation.original_text: “Related Company” shall mean any corporation, company, or other entity which at the time of disclosure of Confidential Information: a) is controlled by a party hereto; b) Controls a party hereto; c) or is under common Control with a party hereto. For this purpose, “Control ” means that more than fifty percent (50%) of the controlled entity’s shares or ownership interest representing the right to make decisions for such entity that are owned or controlled, directly or indirectly, by the controlling entity.
perturbation.changed_text: “Related Company” shall mean any corporation, company, or other entity which at the time of disclosure of Confidential Information: a) is controlled by a party hereto; b) Controls a party hereto; c) or is under common Control with a party hereto. For this purpose, “Control” means that more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the controlled entity’s shares or ownership interest representing the right to make decisions for such entity that are owned or controlled, directly or indirectly, by the controlling entity.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Control' requires more than 50% ownership. The modified definition lowers the threshold to more than 25%. This creates a conflict because entities that previously wouldn't have been considered 'Related Companies' now might be, affecting the scope of confidentiality obligations. This is particularly contradictory, because now related companies can have access to confidential information to disclose to its employees, agents, and consultants based on the new 25% threshold.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use reasonable care, but in no event less than the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information. Each party shall only have the right to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees, agents and consultants on a “need to know” basis; provided, however, that disclosure in any event shall only be made to such persons who have agreed in writing to protect the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party’s information. Each party shall, prior to disclosing any Confidential Information to any such person, issue appropriate instructions to them and obtain all necessary undertakings to ensure that such Persons comply with the confidentiality and use obligations and restrictions contained In this Agreement with respect to the Confidential Information. These obligations shall survive termination or expiry of this Agreement. The Receiving Party may Disclose Confidential Information if required by law, provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior written notice of the requirement and provide reasonable assistance to enable the Disclosing Party to seek protection of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The definition of 'Control' was changed from requiring more than 50% ownership to more than 25%. The contradicted text states that confidential information can be disclosed to employees, agents, and consultants of the Receiving Party, provided they have agreed in writing to protect the confidentiality. With the new definition of 'Control', a company with only 26% ownership could now be considered a 'Related Company'. This means more entities can be given access to confidential information which creates a strong in-text contradiction, because these related companies might not have previously been considered as such, affecting the scope of confidentiality obligations and increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure, contradicting the original intention of limiting access to confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 3.02 Termination. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (30) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.02 Termination. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (5) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original termination clause requires 30 business days' notice. The modified clause requires only 5 business days' notice. This directly conflicts with Section 3.01 regarding the agreement remaining in place for 730 days and changes the nature of the termination conditions. The contract can now be terminated sooner with the new 5 day threshold. Due to this being a shorter period now, the Receiving Party’s obligation to protect the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information received prior to Termination is now also impacted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This agreement shall be effective from the Effective Date until terminated by either party in writing as provided for hereinbelow. And it shall hold good for two years (730 days) starting when both sides sign. The Receiving Party’s obligation to protect the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information received prior to Termination shall survive termination or expiration as stated in Section 2.04. Confidential Information remains Confidential Information as defined.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires a 30 business days notice for termination, while the changed text reduces this to 5 business days. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states the agreement holds good for 730 days and can only be terminated 'as provided for hereinbelow'. The altered termination period directly impacts the duration of the agreement and contradicts the implicitly agreed upon time frame of 730 days within which the Receiving Party must protect the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information received prior to Termination, therefore, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.01 Notices. Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made if delivered by facsimile, by overnight courier or by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, and shall be effective (a) upon receipt if delivered personally; (b) on the business day the notice is received by facsimile; (c) one business day after being sent by overnight courier; and (d) three (3) business days after being deposited in mail, postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.01 Notices. Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made if delivered only by registered mail, return receipt requested, and shall be effective five (5) business days after being deposited in mail, postage prepaid.
perturbation.explanation: The original notice clause allows for notice via facsimile, overnight courier, or certified mail. The modified clause restricts notice to only registered mail. This contradicts with Section 3.02, because now termination can only be made via registered mail, while the agreement doesn't state a format for the termination notice.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (5) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for notice via facsimile, overnight courier, or certified mail, while the changed text restricts notice to only registered mail. The contradicted text (Section 3.02) allows either party to terminate the agreement by giving written notice, without specifying a particular method of delivery. Because the changed text limits acceptable methods of delivery to registered mail only, this contradicts the contradicted text by implication, because termination notices are now limited to registered mail, when they weren't before.

perturbation.original_text: 3.03 Return. Each party shall immediately upon the Termination of this Agreement or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party, discontinue use of the Confidential Information of the other and, if requested by the Disclosing Party, return same and all copies thereof which may be or have been in such party’s direct or indirect possession or control. If return is not requested, the Confidential Information shall be destroyed within ten (10) Business Days of the Termination of the Agreement and an officer’s certificate to that effect provided by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.03 Return. Each party shall, within thirty (30) Business Days of the Termination of this Agreement or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party, return same and all copies thereof which may be or have been in such party’s direct or indirect possession or control. If return is not requested, the Confidential Information shall be destroyed within ten (10) Business Days of the Termination of the Agreement and an officer’s certificate to that effect provided by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original section 3.03 states that after the termination of the agreement, each party shall immediately discontinue use of the confidential information. The modified version provides a thirty business day leeway after termination to return confidential information. This conflicts with the intended immediacy and thoroughness implied by the original agreement and section 2.04, because now the obligations of the confidentiality obligations and restrictions have an added 30 business days.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees to use reasonable care, but in no event less than the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information. Each party shall only have the right to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees, agents and consultants on a “need to know” basis; provided, however, that disclosure in any event shall only be made to such persons who have agreed in writing to protect the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party’s information. Each party shall, prior to disclosing any Confidential Information to any such person, issue appropriate instructions to them and obtain all necessary undertakings to ensure that such Persons comply with the confidentiality and use obligations and restrictions contained In this Agreement with respect to the Confidential Information. These obligations shall survive termination or expiry of this Agreement. The Receiving Party may Disclose Confidential Information if required by law, provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior written notice of the requirement and provide reasonable assistance to enable the Disclosing Party to seek protection of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text required immediate discontinuation of confidential information use upon termination. The changed text allows for a 30-day period to return the information. This directly contradicts the 'immediate' obligation and creates a conflict with the contradicted text, which requires continuous protection of confidential information. The change introduces a 30-day window where the confidentiality obligations are weakened, creating a contradiction according to the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 2.04 Non-Disclosure. The Receiving Party agrees to use reasonable care, but in no event less than the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.04 Non-Disclosure. The Receiving Party agrees to use minimal effort to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires 'reasonable care, but in no event less than the same degree of care' for the Receiving Party to protect the Confidential Information. The modified clause weakens this obligation to 'minimal effort'. This is a contradiction because the Agreement can state that breach is subjected to irreparable damage in section 3.04, while the Receiving Party is being asked to put minimal effort in preventing the breach. The modified text is much less specific and enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party agrees that any breach of this Agreement may give rise to irreparable damage to the other party, the injury to the other party from any such breach would be difficult to calculate, and that money damages would therefore be an inadequate remedy for that breach. Each party agrees that the other party will be entitled, in addition to all other remedies that the other party may have and without showing or proving any actual damage sustained by it, to seek an injunction or other order to restrain any breach, threatened breach or the continuation of any breach of this section.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated 'reasonable care, but in no event less than the same degree of care' to safeguard Confidential Information. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'minimal effort'. Section 3.04 states that any breach of the agreement may cause irreparable damage and money is an inadequate remedy, implying a high standard of care should be taken. The modified clause lowers the standard of care to 'minimal effort', directly contradicting the implication in section 3.04 that a high degree of care should be exercised to prevent breaches.
